---
title: "Paper 3: Supervisor Perspectives on Authoring Tooling in Doctoral Supervision: A Qualitative Study"
---

::: {.contribution}
I designed the interview protocol, recruited and interviewed all 15 supervisor participants, transcribed the interviews, conducted the primary thematic analysis, and wrote the manuscript. Co-author C served as a second independent coder for inter-rater reliability and reviewed the manuscript.
:::

## Abstract

Semi-structured interviews with 15 doctoral supervisors at three UK business schools examined how supervisors interpret and respond to candidate use of policy-compliant authoring tools. Three themes emerged: *delegation of compliance work*, *displacement of intellectual conversation*, and *re-distribution of authority over thesis structure*.

## Introduction

Papers 1 and 2 establish that policy-compliant tooling is increasingly adopted and that adoption causally improves writing quality. But the quantitative work cannot illuminate the *meaning* of these tools for the supervisory relationship. This paper addresses that gap through qualitative interviews.

## Methods

### Sampling

A purposive sample of 15 supervisors was recruited from three universities, balanced by gender, career stage, and disciplinary sub-field. Sampling continued until thematic saturation was reached, following @brown2023qualitative.

### Data collection

Semi-structured interviews of 45--75 minutes were conducted via video conference and audio-recorded with consent.

### Analysis

Transcripts were coded using reflexive thematic analysis. The candidate and Co-author C independently coded the first five transcripts; coding inconsistencies were resolved through discussion, after which the candidate completed the remaining coding.

## Findings

### Theme: delegation of compliance work

Supervisors framed compliance tooling as a "delegation" that freed the supervisor-candidate conversation from procedural concerns:

> Before, I would spend half of our meeting going over the declaration template and the copyright statement. Now the tool does that. We can actually talk about the argument.
>
> --- Supervisor 7, senior professor

### Theme: displacement of intellectual conversation

A minority of supervisors expressed concern that the tools could *displace* intellectual conversation by making structural choices invisible:

> The candidate doesn't even see the decisions the tool is making. There's a risk that they internalise these conventions without ever interrogating them.
>
> --- Supervisor 11, mid-career

### Theme: re-distribution of authority

The most consistent theme was a perceived shift in authority over thesis structure from the supervisor to the institution (via the tool).

## Discussion

The findings complement the quantitative results in Papers 1 and 2 by showing that the *meaning* of tooling adoption is contested. The implication for institutional policy is that tool deployment should be accompanied by supervisor training that addresses these tensions directly.

## Limitations

The sample is restricted to three universities and to business and management disciplines.
